ad_copy: 22 years ago, a woman in Rhode Island asked: what if jewelry meant something? It still does.
brand_visual_direction: Warm, tactile, and deeply grounded in craft. This variation foregrounds the physical act of making — fingerprints on copper, wood grain of a worktable, natural morning light in a New England workshop. The grade is warm amber-gold with deep, rich shadows. Every frame should feel like it was found, not constructed. The product is incidental to the hands, the light, and the intention behind the work. Lean into analog texture: grain, imperfection, the weight of real materials.
generation_prompt: Cinematic heritage brand film, 16:9, warm amber grade with deep shadows and analog grain. Open on an extreme close-up of a single copper wire bending between bare human hands at a worn wooden worktable — macro lens, visible fingerprints, the slight spring-tension of the metal, complete silence except the faint metallic whisper of bending wire. No music. Camera pulls slowly back to reveal a craftsperson in a brick-and-timber workshop in Cranston, Rhode Island — tall factory windows casting low morning light across the worktable. A charm bangle rests naturally on the craftsperson's wrist, unworn as adornment but lived-in. Cut to the brick exterior of a New England building. A woman reads a handwritten meaning card, unposed, private — the camera stays with her face. Overhead shot of bangles, copper wire, and folded meaning cards arranged naturally on the worktable. Warm montage: five wrist shots of women in motion — a kitchen, a car window, a garden, a desk, a morning coffee — bangles stacked, caught mid-gesture, never posed. Return to workshop: a craftsperson holds a glass butterfly beaded bangle up to the tall window — it scatters a constellation of refracted light across the worn wood surface. Three intimate close-ups: three women, three ages, three wrists. None performing for camera. Ends on a cut to a dark recycled copper surface — warm, deep, almost tactile — with the Alex and Ani logo in gold and 'Est. 2004' beneath it. Held in silence. Inspired by Patagonia origin films and analog documentary portraiture.

scenes.camera_angle: Low, slightly angled — wire at center frame, hands partially visible
scenes.camera_movement: Static. Absolute stillness. No camera movement.
scenes.framing: Wire fills two-thirds of the frame. Fingertips at edges. Grain visible in the copper surface.
scenes.key_light: Soft natural window light from frame-left — low morning sun, not direct
scenes.light_quality: Diffused, warm, slightly hazy — as if through old glass
scenes.subject: A single copper wire being bent between two bare hands on a wooden worktable
scenes.action: Slow, deliberate bend. The wire resists slightly. Fingerprint impressions visible on the metal.
scenes.expression: Hands only — no face. The tension in the grip communicates everything.
scenes.background: Worn wooden worktable surface — close enough that only grain and warm amber tones are visible. Soft bokeh.
scenes.depth_of_field: Extremely shallow — wire in crisp focus, everything else falls off rapidly
scenes.color_palette: Copper amber, warm cream, deep walnut brown
scenes.color_grade: Rich, warm, slightly desaturated — pulled toward amber. High local contrast on the wire.
scenes.music: None. Complete silence except the faint metallic sound of the wire bending.

scenes.shot_type: Wide pull-back revealing full workshop, then cut to brick exterior
scenes.camera_angle: Eye-level wide shot from Scene 1's macro — slow pull-back. Exterior cut: low angle looking up at brick facade.
scenes.camera_movement: Very slow, deliberate dolly back — unhurried. Cut to static exterior shot.
scenes.framing: Craftsperson centered at a long worktable, windows behind. Exterior: full building facade, sky above.
scenes.key_light: Low-angle morning sun through tall factory windows — rakes across the worktable surface
scenes.light_quality: Hard-edged window light, warm golden-hour quality despite being morning
scenes.subject: Craftsperson at worktable; then brick exterior of the Cranston, RI workshop building
scenes.action: Craftsperson works with hands, unhurried. Attitude of Gratitude bangle on their wrist — worn naturally, not presented. Cut to static exterior.
scenes.expression: Absorbed in the work. Not performing.
scenes.background: Warm brick walls, timber beams, tall paned windows. Natural clutter of a working studio.
scenes.depth_of_field: Medium — craftsperson sharp, background slightly soft but readable
scenes.color_palette: Warm amber, faded brick red, aged timber, cream
scenes.color_grade: Warmer push in shadows. Highlights pulled slightly toward golden. Analog grain layer added.
scenes.voiceover: There was a question she kept asking.
scenes.music: Single acoustic guitar fingerpick — one note, tentative, like the beginning of a thought.
scenes.text_overlay: Cranston, Rhode Island. 2004. — lower third, small serif typeface, warm gold color, fades in on the exterior cut

scenes.shot_type: Medium close-up on face; cut to overhead flat lay
scenes.camera_angle: Eye-level face shot, slight natural tilt. Overhead: directly above worktable.
scenes.camera_movement: Face shot: static with micro-reframe drift — as if handheld but deeply settled. Overhead: completely static.
scenes.framing: Face in left-of-center frame, natural headroom. Overhead: worktable fills frame edge to edge.
scenes.key_light: Soft window light from one side — gentle wrap, strong cheekbone shadow
scenes.light_quality: Diffused, intimate — like candlelight without the flicker
scenes.subject: A woman reading a meaning card, then overhead of bangles, copper wire, meaning cards on worktable
scenes.action: She reads. Her expression shifts slightly — recognition, or something close to it. Overhead: objects arranged naturally, not staged.
scenes.expression: Unguarded. Private. Not performing for camera.
scenes.background: Soft, indistinct — warm tones. Studio or domestic setting.
scenes.depth_of_field: Shallow on face — background falls off. Overhead: even, all objects sharp.
scenes.color_palette: Skin tones, warm ivory, copper, faded gold
scenes.color_grade: Gentle warmth, slight lift in shadows to keep it soft. Overhead: cooler by one stop to ground the still life.
scenes.voiceover: What if a piece of jewelry could carry something real? Not just gold. Not just silver. Something you'd actually feel.
scenes.music: Single melodic guitar phrase — three to four notes, resolving softly.

scenes.shot_type: Montage — five wrist close-ups and medium shots of women in motion
scenes.camera_angle: Varied per cut: natural, observational — no two cuts from the same angle
scenes.camera_movement: Each cut: slight natural movement — a loose handheld that follows action organically. No stabilization.
scenes.framing: Wrists and hands incidental to the full gesture — woman reaching for a mug, leaning out a car window, kneeling in a garden, typing, lifting a coffee cup.
scenes.key_light: Varied per location: warm kitchen window, warm car-window sidelight, garden overcast, desk lamp, morning café light
scenes.light_quality: Each shot has its own natural quality — the unifying thread is warmth, not uniformity
scenes.light_mood: Alive, present, unperformed. Life in motion.
scenes.subject: Five women in motion — the bangles are incidental. Women are the subject. Token of Love bangle and Glass Butterfly bangle appear among the stacked wrists.
scenes.action: Natural gestures: reaching, turning, gardening, typing, drinking coffee. Bangles catch light mid-motion. Never held toward camera.
scenes.expression: Not looking at camera. Engaged in their own moment.
scenes.background: Varied, naturalistic: warm kitchen, car interior, garden green, desk workspace, café
scenes.depth_of_field: Medium — enough context to feel real. Shallow enough that bangles have intimacy.
scenes.color_palette: Across cuts: warm kitchen amber, cool car window blue, garden green-gold, soft office neutral, café cream
scenes.color_grade: Each shot retains its location temperature — subtle grade unifies without flattening. Warm shadow base throughout.
scenes.music: Second acoustic guitar layer enters almost imperceptibly — a counter-melody, fingerpicked, soft.

scenes.shot_type: Medium close-up — hand holding bangle to window; then static hold on light scatter
scenes.camera_angle: Slightly low — hand in frame below the window's light source. Light scatter falls on worktable.
scenes.camera_movement: Slow subtle push toward the light scatter as camera holds — barely perceptible. Feels like leaning in.
scenes.framing: Hand and bangle in upper half; refracted light points across worktable surface below.
scenes.key_light: Direct morning window light — used as a backlight source through the glass butterfly beads
scenes.light_quality: Hard source through semi-translucent glass — creates point-source reflections that scatter across the wood
scenes.light_mood: Wonder. Quiet revelation. The payoff of all the craft that preceded it.
scenes.subject: Craftsperson's hand holding the Glass Butterfly beaded charm bangle up to the tall window
scenes.action: The bangle is raised slowly to the window. Light catches the glass beads and scatters across the worktable. Camera holds on the light scatter for two full seconds.
scenes.expression: Hand only. The stillness of the hand communicates intention.
scenes.background: Tall factory window, morning sky beyond — overexposed, white. Worktable surface below, warm amber grain.
scenes.depth_of_field: Split — bangle slightly soft, light scatter on worktable in sharp focus
scenes.color_palette: White window wash, warm amber wood, scattered prismatic light points, copper
scenes.color_grade: Slight highlights blow on the window — intentional. Warm, slightly lifted shadows on the wood surface.
scenes.voiceover: She just wanted to make something that meant something.
scenes.music: Guitar sustains — single chord held, not resolved.

scenes.shot_type: Three intimate close-ups — three wrists, three women
scenes.camera_angle: Each from a different angle: one from above, one straight on, one slight low angle
scenes.camera_movement: First wrist: completely static. Second: very slight drift, barely perceptible. Third: static, then a slow fade begins.
scenes.framing: Wrist and hand. Enough context to feel human — not a product shot. Lap, tabletop, side.
scenes.key_light: Soft, varied — window light, ambient indoor, natural shade. Each woman in her own environment.
scenes.light_quality: Gentle, intimate, not studio. Feels observed rather than lit.
scenes.light_mood: Quiet. Settled. These women are not performing.
scenes.subject: Three women, three ages. Make Your Own Luck Ladybug bangle visible on one wrist. The others wear stacked bangles.
scenes.action: First: glances at her wrist. Second: adjusts a bangle — minor, habitual. Third: holds her wrist in her lap, still.
scenes.expression: None performing for camera. Natural, interior.
scenes.background: Varied, soft — warm blurs of domestic and personal spaces.
scenes.depth_of_field: Shallow — wrists sharp, backgrounds fall away
scenes.color_palette: Skin tones, warm gold, silver, soft ambient backgrounds
scenes.color_grade: Slightly cooled from Scene 5 — more neutral, grounding the emotion. Shadows kept warm.
scenes.music: Guitar fades to a single held note — barely there. One breath of sound.

scenes.shot_type: Static card — brand identity on textured surface
scenes.framing: Logo centered. Text below. Textured surface fills the entire frame — nothing else.
scenes.key_light: None — the surface itself provides the depth. Ambient light only, barely there.
scenes.light_quality: Flat and absorbed — the texture of recycled metal holds the light rather than reflecting it
scenes.light_mood: Complete quiet. A period at the end of a long sentence.
scenes.subject: Alex and Ani logo in warm gold. 'Est. 2004' beneath in small serif. Dark, textured recycled copper surface.
scenes.action: Nothing moves. Three-second hold in silence.
scenes.background: The recycled metal surface IS the background. Deep, warm, almost tactile. No product in frame.
scenes.depth_of_field: Deep — entire surface in focus. The texture is the visual point.
scenes.color_palette: Deep warm black, aged copper undertones, gold
scenes.color_grade: Darkest grade of the film. Warm undertones in the blacks. Logo retains gold warmth.
scenes.text_overlay: Alex and Ani logo — centered, warm gold. 'Est. 2004' — small, below, same gold. No animation.

style_references: Patagonia 'The Mountain' origin film — long-form brand heritage, craft-first framing
Warby Parker founding story format — single founding question as narrative spine
Apple product launch silence-first film grammar — first 3 seconds with no music
Documentary portraiture in the tradition of Diane Arbus and Sally Mann — unperformed subjects
Analog 16mm film grain aesthetic — not digital clean
negative_prompts: Jewelry held toward camera or presented for display — product must always appear worn or incidental
Over-saturated colors or artificially vibrant tones that undermine the warm, muted heritage palette
Fast cuts, jump cuts, or energetic pacing — this film breathes slowly and deliberately
Smiling for camera, posed expressions, or any talent performing awareness of being filmed
Digital-clean, sterile, or clinical visual quality — all imperfection and grain is intentional
Morphing faces, extra fingers, distorted hands, uncanny skin texture or AI-artifact creep in close-up hand shots
Any visual element that reads as contemporary fast-fashion or trend-driven — this must feel timeless
post_production_notes: Edit rhythm: Scene 1 to Scene 2 is the only hard cut — everything else dissolves or breathes. Hold each scene slightly longer than feels comfortable. The discomfort is the point.
Color grade: Base LUT should pull toward warm amber with rich, textured shadows — think aged Kodak film stock, not modern digital warmth. Never clip shadows to pure black; keep detail in the darks.
Grain: Apply analog film grain at a moderate intensity, consistent across all scenes. Scene 7 should have the densest grain — the texture must feel physical.

Music mix: The acoustic guitar must enter so softly in Scene 2 that a first-time viewer might not register it. It builds in presence across Scenes 3-5 before retreating to a single held note in Scene 6. Scene 7 is absolute silence — no room tone, no fade-out. A true cut to silence.

Voiceover: Female voice, warm and unhurried — mid-register, no vocal fry, no performance affect. Pacing should feel like someone speaking to a friend, not addressing an audience. Record multiple takes at conversational volume.
Text overlay (Scene 2): 'Cranston, Rhode Island. 2004.' — should appear and hold for 4 seconds, then dissolve. Use a small, light-weight serif. Warm gold. Never white.
Scene 5 light scatter: If compositing is required to enhance the refracted light points, keep them physically plausible — no lens flares, no added glow. The light should look like physics, not magic.

Sustainability messaging integration: No explicit eco copy in this variation. The sustainability message lives in the materiality — copper wire, recycled texture of Scene 7 surface, the emphasis on made-to-last craft. Let the visuals carry it.

scene_2.treatment: Worn naturally on craftsperson's wrist — visible but never presented. Camera does not direct attention to it.

scene_3.treatment: Overhead environmental shot — bangles among meaning cards and copper wire on worktable. Not arranged for display.

scene_4.product: Token of Love Charm Bangle, Glass Butterfly Beaded Charm Bangle
scene_4.treatment: Appear among stacked bangles on wrists in motion montage. Never isolated or highlighted.

scene_5.treatment: Held to window light — this is the one moment a product is the primary visual subject. The light scatter is the payoff.

scene_6.product: Make Your Own Luck Ladybug Charm Bangle
scene_6.treatment: Visible on one of the three wrists in the intimate close-up sequence. Not the focus — the woman is the focus.

sustainability_notes: This variation embeds sustainability entirely within the craft aesthetic — the recycled copper wire in Scene 1, the worn materiality of the workshop, and the textured recycled metal surface in the brand card (Scene 7) all communicate longevity and anti-fast-fashion values without explicit copy. The emphasis on a single craftsperson, a single wire, and the slow accumulation of 22 years implicitly argues for made-to-last over made-to-replace.
